title-group.article-title: Functional brain alterations in anxious depression: Insights from whole-brain fMRI and meta-analysis

contrib-group.aff: aSchool of Clinical Medicine, The First Affiliated Hospital of Chengdu Medical College, Chengdu, China

bDepartment of Radiology, Imaging Institute of Rehabilitation and Development of Brain Function, The Second Clinical Medical College of North Sichuan Medical College Nanchong Central Hospital, Nanchong, China

event.event-desc: published online in a building issue

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Author(s). Published by Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group.

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/), which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited. The terms on which this article has been published allow the posting of the Accepted Manuscript in a repository by the author(s) or with their consent.

sec.title: Objective
sec.p: The aim of our study was to investigate neurobiological markers and gender difference for diagnosing anxious major depressive disorder (aMDD) through a meta-analysis.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: We systematically searched multiple databases for whole-brain neuroimaging studies comparing anxious major depression disorder (aMDD), pure MDD, and healthy controls, with publication dates through December 2024. We extracted brain coordinates and their corresponding peaks, for further analysis using the Seed-based d Mapping software package.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: A total of 11 studies were included in this study, encompassing 829 aMDD patients, 681 MDD patients, and 865 healthy controls. The meta-analysis revealed that aMDD patients exhibited increased functional alteration in the left middle temporal gyrus compared to individuals diagnosed with MDD. In the comparison between individuals with aMDD and healthy controls, the meta-analysis revealed increased functional alteration in the anterior commissure and decreased functional alteration the right middle frontal gyrus. Furthermore, the meta-regression analysis revealed heightened neural activity of the left middle cingulate gyrus and right anterior thalamic regions, as well as weakened neural activity of the left rolandic operculum in females with aMDD compared to the control group.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: We identified specific functional alterations in brain regions that may serve as potential neurobiological markers for aMDD and associated differences.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Major depressive disorder (MDD), a prevalent psychiatric condition, is characterised by core clinical manifestations including persistent depressed mood, anhedonia, and fatigue. Some patients exhibit noticeable anxiety and agitation, leading to cognitive dysfunction, hallucinations, delusions and more (Kendler et al. 1995; Kessler et al. 2006; Nemeroff and Goldschmidt-Clermont 2012). In 2017, depression affected 322 million people worldwide, approximately 4.3% of the population, and is the leading cause of disability globally (Li et al. 2018). Existing studies have considered MDD to be a heterogeneous disease with multiple subtypes, among which a common subtype is anxious major depressive disorder(aMDD). Compared with MDD or anxiety disorder alone, aMDD has lower treatment efficacy, longer illness duration, greater impairment in quality of life, and an increased suicide risk (Brown et al. 1996; Middeldorp et al. 2006, 2008; Papakostas and Larsen 2011; Goldberg and Fawcett 2012). The concept of personalised medicine, which involves tailoring targeted therapies to match subdiagnostic specificity, represents a comprehensive approach for various conditions, including MDD. This approach holds the potential to enhance treatment efficiency, effectiveness, and durability. A viable strategy towards achieving this objective is to refine the identification of meaningful subtypes within MDD due to its inherent heterogeneity (Agosti and Stewart 2001; Sullivan et al. 2002).

Although fMRI-based research on MDD and anxiety disorders has been extensive, studies focusing on their comorbid conditions (e.g., aMDD) remain limited, and no clear consensus exists on their underlying neurobiological markers (Raichle 2015). The cingulate gyrus, which was typically hyperactivated in MDD due to heightened vigilance towards reward anticipation, did not exhibit the same pattern of hyperactivation in individuals with aMDD (Gorka et al. 2014). Meanwhile, another study (Waugh et al. 2012) found that cingulate gyrus activity exhibited greater intensity in MDD patients than in aMDD patients. Some scholars (Andreescu et al. 2011) found that there is a pattern of separation of the default mode network (DMN) in aMDD patients. The functional connectivity of the region behind the DMN, including occipital and parietal lobe association area, was significantly enhanced compared with non-aMDD patients, while the regions in front of DMN including the coracoid anterior cingulate gyrus, medial prefrontal cortex and orbitofrontal cortex, were significantly weakened compared with non-aMDD patients. Pannekoek et al. (2015) found that in aMDD patients, there were two clusters of functional connections in the fronto-parietal network (one on the bilateral anterior cingulate cortex, insula, and angular gyrus; the other on the right middle frontal gyrus, inferior parietal lobule, and middle frontal gyrus), which were stronger than those in the normal control group. No significant differences in resting-state functional connectivity were observed in other brain networks and in other patient groups (MDD/anxiety), which suggesting that some fronto-parietal network functional connectivity may be specific to aMDD patients. However, Altinay et al. (2016) found that the functional connectivity between the amygdala and the cerebral cortex, as well as between the amygdala and pons, was weaker in the aMDD group compared to the healthy control group. Two studies of the limbic-cortical network used different methods; the first used independent component analysis, while the second used functional connectivity between the amygdala as the region of interest (ROI) and other regions of the brain, measured using different metrics, which may have some impact on the results. Additionally, Sindermann et al. (2022) discovered in their task-based study that the bilateral insula and medial and inferior frontal gyrus exhibited significant activation in patients with aMDD compared to those with MDD.

Anxiety and depression exhibit notable gender disparities, with a prevalence rate in females that is approximately twice that observed in males (Kessler et al. 1994, 2005). Neuroimaging research had demonstrated that, when processing negative emotional faces, females exhibited significantly greater amygdala activation compared to males. Conversely, the activation of the left amygdala in response to positive emotions was markedly more pronounced in males than in females (Stevens and Hamann 2012). In addition to gender differences in emotion-related brain activation, there were also significant gender differences in functional connectivity between associated brain regions during negative emotional processing. One study (Lungu et al. 2015) revealed that males exhibited notably stronger functional connectivity from the right amygdala to the dorsomedial anterior frontal cortex compared to females. This indicated that emotional processing may occur through sex-specific neural mechanisms in both males and females. Simultaneously, the anterior frontal cortex and insular cortices play crucial roles in the processing of emotional information. Dysfunction within the anterior frontal cortex, which governs emotional regulation, decision-making, executive function, and attention control, may impair an individual’s cognitive evaluation and responsiveness to emotional stimuli. The insula plays a crucial role in self-awareness, interoception, and cognitive control, and its dysfunction can disrupt the integration and modulation of emotional and somatic experiences. A research had demonstrated significant gender differences in the processing of emotional information within both the prefrontal and insular cortices (Wager et al. 2003). Li et al. (2022) conducted an fMRI study and found that in the female sample, the aMDD group exhibited significantly lower fractional amplitude of low-frequency fluctuation (fALFF) values in the bilateral anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) and bilateral insula compared to both the MDD group and the control group, whereas no significant differences were observed in the male sample. However, another study (Zhang et al. 2016) revealed that female patients with MDD exhibited significantly increased regional homogeneity (ReHo) values in the left ACC, alongside significantly decreased ReHo values in the right putamen, left middle frontal gyrus, and left middle occipital gyrus. Therefore, it can be concluded that sex differences exist in the neural mechanisms of anxious depression (aMDD), although there is currently no consensus regarding specific brain regions involved.

Extensive evidence suggests that aMDD demonstrates distinct neural activity patterns compared to non-comorbid MDD. This study aims to (1) identify characteristic neurofunctional abnormalities in aMDD, (2) examine potential gender differences in these neural signatures, and (3) establish robust neurobiological markers to facilitate more precise diagnosis and personalised treatment strategies.

sec.title: Method
sec.p: We searched a total of 6523 citations in PubMed, Medline, Embase and Cochrane databases by using the terms: (fMRI OR functional MRI OR functional magnetic resonance imaging OR neuroimaging OR functional imaging OR functional magnetic imaging) AND (anxious MDD OR anxious depression OR anxious depressive disorders) through 19 December 2024. These articles were ultimately selected for inclusion in our study, resulting in a total of 11 citations, as determined by two radiologists. Figure 1 illustrates the detailed exclusion process. The inclusion criteria were as follows: (1) We selected studies that utilised whole-brain measures, while those employing ROI approaches were excluded. (2) The study also incorporated the comparative results between aMDD and MDD, as well as the healthy controls for aMDD. (3) The articles reported the brain coordinates exhibiting statistical significance along with their corresponding peak values. Studies using ROI analysis were excluded because this approach violated the null hypothesis of equal likelihood of locating active focal points/clusters among participants (Albajes-Eizagirre et al. 2019). The null hypothesis posits that the effect size, not merely the peak values, would be randomly distributed throughout the brain (Radua et al. 2012). Additionally, the 23 excluded studies were removed for the following reasons: (1) authors did not report peak coordinates of significant findings, and (2) some studies focused solely on structural brain measurements.

caption.p: Flow chart showing study selection process.

sec.title: Data analysis
sec.p: The full text was carefully reviewed by two radiologists (H.H and LZ.Q), who extracted general information about the study and statistical differences in brain regions (Table 1). Next, we split the data into two groups: (aMDD vs. MDD) and (aMDD vs. Healthy control). We reconstructed the peak coordinate converting t value into Hedge’s effect sizes by ‘Seed-based d Mapping with Permutation of Subject Images’ (SDM-PSI) (Albajes-Eizagirre et al. 2019). Anisotropic Gaussian kernels were applied to weight voxels based on their spatial correlation with peak coordinates, assigning larger effect sizes to voxels showing stronger peak-related activation. To maintain directional specificity, only positive activations were included in the projection. We implemented a random-effects model to account for both within-study and between-study variability, as well as sample size differences across studies. The threshold in the random effects model was set as p = 0.05 (FDR corrected).

caption.p: General information of all included studies.

fn.p: N1: sample size of aMDD group; N2: sample size of non-aMDD group; N3: sample size of healthy control; MCG: middle cingulate gyrus; PCG: paracingulate gyri; ACC: anterior cingulate cortex; CG: cingulate gyrus; SFG: superior frontal gyrus; MFG: middle frontal gyrus; IPG: inferior parietal gyrus; STG: superior temporal gyrus; postCG: postcentral gyrus; IFG: inferior frontal gyrus; preCG: precentral gyrus; SOG: superior occipital gyrus; MTG: middle temporal gyrus; ITG: inferior temporal gyrus; SPG: superior parietal gyrus; FC: functional connectivity; ALFF: amplitude of low-frequency fluctuation; VMHC: voxel-mirrored homotopic connectivity; fALFF: fractional functional amplitude of low-frequency fluctuations.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Our analysis focused on the aMDD group, with comparative analyses against both the MDD group and healthy controls, revealing significant between-group differences in neural activity patterns. In subsequent sex-stratified analyses, regression models demonstrated significant neural activity differences between aMDD and HC groups in female participants, but not between aMDD and MDD groups. Table 2 presented all the between-group comparison results from this meta-analysis.

fn.p: L: left; R: right; MTG: middle temporal gyrus; AC: anterior commissure; MFG: middle frontal gyrus.

sec.title: Comparison between aMDD vs. MDD
sec.p: In our analysis comparing individuals with aMDD to MDD, the meta-analysis revealed a significant difference in the left middle temporal gyrus (p = 0.020) (Figure 2). This finding suggests that the left middle temporal gyrus shows enhanced functional changes in aMDD patients compared to those with MDD alone.

caption.p: Comparison between aMDD vs. MDD showed significantly increased functional alteration in the left medial frontal gyrus.

sec.title: Comparison between aMDD vs. healthy control
sec.p: The meta-analysis comparing aMDD patients with healthy controls revealed significant neural alterations in two distinct regions: the anterior commissure showed hyperactivation (p = 0.004), while the right middle frontal gyrus exhibited functional suppression (p = 0.028) (Figure 3). These findings suggest a complex pattern of both enhanced and reduced neural activity characterising aMDD relative to neurotypical controls.

caption.p: Comparison between aMDD vs. healthy control showed significantly increased functional alteration in the anterior commissure (A) and decreased functional alteration the right middle frontal gyrus (B).

sec.title: Comparison between MDD vs. healthy control
sec.p: Among the included studies, four (Peng et al. 2019; Hou et al. 2023; Beckmann et al. 2024; Zhou et al. 2024) primarily focused on anxious depression, anxiety disorders, and MDD, and thus did not report results comparing MDD groups with healthy control groups. To ensure the comprehensiveness of the research findings, this meta-analysis extracted and re-analyzed data from the remaining seven studies that included comparisons between the MDD group and the control group. The results indicated no significant differences between the two groups.

sec.title: Meta-regression analysis
sec.p: Anxiety and depression exhibited notable gender disparities, with these conditions being approximately twice as prevalent in women compared to men (Kessler et al. 1994; 2005). Therefore, we employed meta-regression analysis to identify that, compared with the control group, female patients in the aMDD group exhibited significantly increased functional characteristic in brain regions (Table 3), including the left medial cingulate gyrus (p = 0.018), right anterior thalamic projections (p = 0.034), certain neural circuit (p = 0.022), while decreased functional characteristic in the left rolandic operculum (p = 0.049) (Figure 4). Furthermore, the regression analysis between the aMDD and MDD groups did not yield any statistically significant findings.

caption.p: Results of meta-regression analysis between aMDD and the healthy control: significant functional characteristic including the left medial cingulate gyrus (A), right anterior thalamic regions (B), left the rolandic operculum (E) and more (C and D).

fn.p: L: left; R: right; ACG: anterior central gyrus; ATP: anterior thalamic projections; SLF: superior longitudinal fasciculus; RO: rolandic operculum.

sec.title: Bias test
sec.p: We conducted a bias analysis of the included literature and found the significance of bias to be 0.728 (Bias: −0.31; z: −0.35; df: 9; p: 0.728) (Figure 5), indicating a low risk of bias in the included studies.

sec.title: Heterogeneity analysis
sec.p: We performed a heterogeneity analysis on the included studies, and the results were as follows: (T = 0.022, Q = 16.589, df = 10, p = 0.084), indicating a low level of heterogeneity among the studies. Since the comparison between the MDD group and the healthy control group was based on only seven studies, a heterogeneity test was conducted again for these seven articles. The results were as follows: (T = 0.000, Q = 8.010, df = 6, p = 0.237, indicating no significant heterogeneity among the studies.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: The left middle temporal gyrus exhibited significantly higher activation in the aMDD group compared to the MDD group, a finding that aligned with previous research conclusions. For example, one study (Qi et al. 2014) reported a significant increase in gray matter volume within the left middle temporal gyrus among aMDD patients when compared to MDD patients. Similarly, Zhou et al. (2024) found that aMDD patients demonstrated a significant increase in the fALFF value within this region. The left middle temporal gyrus demonstrates an increased gray matter volume along with heightened neural activity, which may suggest its potential involvement in anxiety disorders (Vythilingam et al. 2000; Uchida et al. 2003; Inkster et al. 2011; Liao et al. 2013). This region, corresponding to Brodmann’s area 22, plays a crucial role in visual perception, audiovisual integration, attentional processes, working memory, as well as memory consolidation (Schienle et al. 2011). Our findings may support the valence-arousal model (Heller et al. 1995), which posits that depression is linked to diminished activity in the right inferior parietal temporal lobe region associated with arousal properties, while anxiety is associated with heightened activity. Meanwhile, the PCC and bilateral temporal lobes are integral components of the default mode network, collectively responsible for memory retrieval related to episodic events in healthy adults (Fox et al. 2005; Hamilton et al. 2015). A PET-based study (Cabeza and Nyberg 2000) indicates that lesions in the temporal lobe may disrupt neuronal connectivity between these regions, leading to aberrant activity within the default mode network (Fransson and Marrelec 2008). Previous meta-analyses (Miller et al. 2015) have consistently reported a significant increase in DMN fALFF in patients with depression, which partially aligns with our analysis findings. Interestingly, a significantly enhanced functional connectivity between the middle temporal gyrus and the parietal lobe has also been observed in patients with chronic insomnia comorbid with anxiety(H. Zhang et al. 2024). Functional alterations in the middle temporal gyrus (MTG) are not only characterised by significant activation in resting-state fMRI. Emerging research (Kamashita et al. 2024) has revealed that the MTG exhibits more pronounced activity in the brains of female patients with anorexia nervosa. Abnormal neural activity in the middle temporal gyrus is evident not only in aMDD but also across a range of psychiatric disorders, including chronic insomnia disorder (Yang et al. 2023), anxiety, Alzheimer’s disease (Zhigang et al. 2011), aphasia (Chunxing et al. 2014), and epilepsy.

In addition, the findings of this study revealed that, compared to the healthy control group, individuals with aMDD exhibited significantly heightened neural activity in the anterior commissure cortex and notably attenuated neural activity in the right middle frontal gyrus.

The anterior commissure responsible for interconnecting the two amygdalae, is primarily implicated in emotional regulation and facilitation of social interactions (Hsu et al. 2020). In response to emotional stimuli, individuals with anxiety disorders exhibit significant amygdala activity (Phan et al. 2006). Interestingly, Altinay et al. (2016) findings revealed that in patients with aMDD, there was a reduction in resting state functional connections between the left amygdala and cerebellum as well as the occipital lobe. Additionally, reduced resting state connections were observed between the right amygdala and frontal lobe, parietal lobe, posterior central gyrus, anterior cuneus, and cerebellum. The findings of this study suggest that the observed phenomenon may be attributed to impaired amygdala functionality in patients with aMDD, resulting in compensatory anterior commissure activity. Furthermore, the anterior commissure may significantly influence a wide range of psychiatric disorders, including but not limited to schizophrenia (David 1994), bipolar disorder (Arnone et al. 2008), anxiety disorders (Etkin et al. 2009), autism spectrum disorders (Fingher et al. 2017), and post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) (Shin et al. 2006). Future research could delve deeper into the specific mechanisms by which the anterior commissure influences depression and investigate innovative therapeutic strategies based on its functionality.

Juan et al. (2024) observed a significant decrease in functional connectivity and gray matter volume among patients with aMDD compared to controls, which aligned with our findings. The right middle frontal gyrus, a component of Brodmann’s area 46, plays a crucial role in the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC), which is responsible for working memory, cognitive control, and attentional processes that involve top-down regulation of emotional processing (Sibon et al. 2007). The impairment of DLPFC has been observed in non-elderly patients with aMDD, suggesting that dysfunctions in top-down processing contribute to both anxiety and depressive symptoms. In contrast, other studies (Talati et al. 2015) have reported an increase in gray matter volume in patients with aMDD compared to non-aMDD and healthy control groups. In adult patients, increased brain volume may be associated with processes such as neuronal differentiation, synaptogenesis, enhanced synaptic connections, and regional blood flow, thereby leading to alterations in the structure of gray matter (Tang et al. 2007; Zou et al. 2010; Wu et al. 2011). Notably, a previous study (Kandilarova et al. 2018) found that MDD patients exhibit inhibited functional connectivity from the anterior insula to the middle frontal gyrus compared to healthy controls. Similarly, another task-based fMRI study (Stoyanov et al. 2021) revealed that during depressive episodes, the brain transmits signals from the anterior insula to the DLPFC. This may suggest that the directionality of neural activity exhibits specificity during the onset of depression. Some clinical studies revealed that aMDD patients exhibited more severe symptoms (Goldberg et al. 2014), greater dysfunction (Goldberg and Fawcett 2012; Zimmerman et al. 2019), and a worse prognosis (Deckert and Erhardt 2019). Considering its distinct clinical features and unique abnormalities in brain function, it was reasonable to hypothesise that aMDD may present additional deviations in brain function which could potentially manifesting as reduced neural activity.

We also observed a significant association between the prevalence of aMDD in females and increased functional alteration of the left cingulate gyrus, right anterior thalamic projections, and right superior longitudinal fasciculus I, as well as inhibitory effects on the left rolandic operculum.

The cingulate gyrus, part of Brodman’s area 23 and an integral component of the limbic-cortical system, is intricately connected to the amygdala, orbitofrontal cortex and hippocampus, and plays a crucial role in regulating emotional processing (Liotti et al. 2002; Wang et al. 2008; Matthews et al. 2009). The rostral cingulate gyrus region primarily participates in the evaluation of emotional and motivational information, whereas the dorsal cingulate gyrus region is involved in effort processing, motivational processes, and regulation of negative emotions (Bush et al. 2000). The ventral cingulate gyrus region (including the rostral cingulate gyrus region) is associated with executive inhibition, evoked sad emotions, and processing of negative emotions in both patients and controls (Mak et al. 2009). The cingulate gyrus has been observed to exhibit heightened neural activity in individuals diagnosed with MDD (Gotlib et al. 2005). The study conducted by Hou et al. (2023) revealed a significantly higher ALFF value in the left cingulate gyrus of the aMDD group compared to the healthy control group, which aligns with our findings. Women exhibited stronger activation in the anterior and middle cingulate gyrus in response to negative images (Wrase et al. 2003), and they demonstrated higher levels of 5-hydroxytryptamine receptor binding in the cingulate cortex compared to men (Biver et al. 1996; Parsey et al. 2002). Our findings indicated that dysfunction of the cingulate gyrus is significantly associated with aMDD in women, regardless of their depressive status or severity of anxiety. This widespread impairment of the cingulate gyrus supported the notion that depression and anxiety share a common aetiology and may stem from a shared pathophysiological mechanism associated with aberrant emotional processing (Mohanty et al. 2007).

The anterior thalamic projections and the superior longitudinal fasciculus are involved in the construction of neural circuits in the brain, with particular emphasis on the anterior thalamic radiation’s integral role in the Papez neural circuit (Mayo et al. 2017). The anterior thalamic radiation serves as the primary neural pathway connecting the thalamus, insula, anterior cingulate gyrus, and orbitofrontal cortex within the anterior cingulate gyrus (Qin et al. 2021). The findings of this study indicate that individuals with aMDD exhibit neural circuitry abnormalities, which align with the pathophysiological changes associated with anxiety or depression (Lingtao 2013). Furthermore, these results suggest a higher likelihood of detecting such abnormalities in females with aMDD. One study (Tao et al. 2023) demonstrated that oestrogen modulates the activity of GABAergic neurons in the medial preoptic area (MPOA), which projects to the ventral tegmental area and dorsal raphe nucleus, thereby influencing a variety of depressive phenotypes. Specifically, it regulates the depressive features of anhedonia and learned helplessness. Clinically, modulating sex hormone levels has been shown to achieve therapeutic benefits in the treatment of anxiety and depressive disorders. Leuprolide, a gonadotropin-like agonist, has demonstrated efficacy in alleviating symptoms associated with premenstrual anxiety (Schmidt et al. 1998).

The left rolandic operculum, classified as Brodman’s 48th region, encompasses the inferior anterior central gyrus, inferior central and inferior posterior central gyrus. It plays a crucial role in language processing and higher cognitive functions (Shan et al. 2021). Voxel-mirrored homotopic connectivity (VMHC) (Jiang et al. 2025) within the precentral gyrus has been found to be significantly reduce in patients with aMDD compared to the control group. Furthermore, when comparing aMDD patients to MDD patients, there was also a significant decrease in VMHC within the precentral gyrus. In a longitudinal study (Mundy et al. 2021), progressive thinning of the cerebral cortex in the precentral gyrus was found to be associated with a higher risk subtype of MDD. The aforementioned statement is in alignment with our research findings. An association between the precentral gyrus and negative attribution bias has been observed in other studies (Blackwood et al. 2000). Additionally, there is strong evidence linking abnormal neural activity in the precentral gyrus to anxiety processing (Pfurtscheller et al. 2018).

Structural MRI studies (Zhang et al. 2018; Chen et al. 2021) have revealed significantly impaired white matter integrity in the corpus callosum, superior longitudinal fasciculus, and thalamic radiations among depressed patients with suicide attempts, compared to both non-attempter depressed patients and healthy controls. Moreover, gray matter alterations in the frontal lobe, paracentral lobule, occipital lobe, and middle temporal gyrus are strongly associated with suicidal behaviour in depressed patients. Notably, volume changes in the middle temporal gyrus demonstrate a dual association—not only correlating with suicidal ideation but also showing significant links to childhood abuse (Zhang et al. 2020; Liu et al. 2021; Wang et al. 2021). The study by Du et al. (2017) revealed that MDD patients with suicidal tendencies exhibit significantly weakened functional connectivity in the anterior cingulate gyrus, orbitomedial PFC, and right middle temporal gyrus compared to both non-suicidal MDD patients and healthy controls. Notably, the connection strength between the anterior cingulate gyrus and the middle temporal gyrus showed a positive correlation with the severity of suicidal ideation. Neuroimaging evidence from amygdala-seeded functional connectivity analyses (Yang et al. 2022) revealed distinct circuit disruptions specific to suicidal depression. Compared to both non-suicidal MDD patients and healthy controls, the suicidal group exhibited significantly impaired functional connectivity between the amygdala and three critical regions: the caudate nucleus (reward processing), middle temporal gyrus (emotional memory), and postcentral gyrus (sensory integration). These findings suggested a unique neural signature of suicide risk that extends beyond general depression pathology.

According to the ICD-11 diagnostic criteria (Lam et al. 2013), MDD is classified as a subgroup of mood disorders, encompassing major types such as single-episode depressive disorder, recurrent depressive disorder, dysthymia, and anxious depression. The DSM-5 system, introduced in 2013, implemented substantial revisions to the existing diagnostic classifications, emphasising the importance of dimensional assessments of comprehensive and critical disease features when establishing a diagnosis (Narrow and Kuhl 2011). Simultaneously, as a revision to the inappropriate diagnostic category (mixed anxiety-depressive disorder) proposed in earlier drafts, DSM-5 added the ‘with anxious distress specifier’ to the diagnostic criteria for depressive disorders (and MDD of bipolar disorder) (Regier et al. 2013). The introduction of this specifier aims to assist clinicians in more effectively evaluating comorbid anxiety symptoms in depressive disorders while preventing the oversight of critical clinical information due to overly rigid diagnostic categorisation. Furthermore, comparative studies on the clinical features of non-anxious depression and anxious depression (Zhiguo 2015) revealed that anxious depressive disorder can be clinically identified both through symptom-based dimensional definitions (i.e., core depressive symptoms with prominent anxiety features) and comorbidity-based definitions (i.e., MDD co-occurring with anxiety disorders). This supports the validity of anxious depression as a distinct diagnostic subtype within mood disorders.

Current clinical diagnosis primarily relies on medical history collection and psychological scale assessments, including: A score of ≥7 on the anxiety/somatization factor of the 17-item Hamilton Depression Rating Scale (HAMD-17), Beck Depression Inventory (BDI), State-Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI-S). Although pharmacotherapy remains the primary clinical intervention for depression, a growing body of recent research (Davidson et al. 2002; Fava et al. 2008; Domschke et al. 2010; Wiethoff et al. 2010) has reported that patients with anxious depression tend to have poorer treatment outcomes compared to those with non-anxious depression. In their randomised controlled trial, Hutton and colleagues (Hutton et al. 2023) reported that when receiving DLPFG-targeted repetitive TMS (DLPFC-rTMS), patients with aMDD exhibited significantly greater reductions (p < 0.01) on standardised psychometric scales compared to MDD controls, with both groups achieving clinically meaningful response rates (≥50% symptom reduction). The findings reported by Hutton et al. were not isolated. Multiple independent studies (Diefenbach et al. 2013; Caulfield and Stern 2020; Pell et al. 2022; Caussat et al. 2025) have consistently demonstrated that TMS targeting the DLPFC induces significant clinical improvement in patients with aMDD.

Functional MRI not only helps elucidate the neurophysiological aetiology of anxious depression but also demonstrates significant potential in studying other neurological disorders. Compared to healthy controls, patients with schizophrenia exhibit significantly hyperactivated prefrontal cortex (PFC) function (Smieskova et al. 2010), with even greater activation intensity than observed in patients with bipolar disorder (Maggioni et al. 2017). Functional amygdala hyperactivity is particularly prominent in adolescent depression (Swartz et al. 2015), potentially demonstrating a high association with adverse family environments (Kaczkurkin et al. 2016; Barbour et al. 2020; Young et al. 2021). Patients with chronic insomnia demonstrate significant functional abnormalities in the cingulate cortex and prefrontal regions. Crucially, the worsening of these neural dysfunctions intensifies chronic anxiety symptoms, which in turn exacerbates the severity of insomnia, forming a vicious cycle (Xu et al. 2024). These findings demonstrate that fMRI can not only identify disorder-specific neural alterations relative to healthy controls, but also possesses discriminative capacity to differentiate between distinct psychiatric conditions to a certain extent.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Abnormal neural activity in the left middle temporal gyrus, anterior commissure, and right middle frontal gyrus is associated with comorbidities of depression and anxiety. These brain regions may serve as neurobiological markers for the diagnosis of aMDD. Specifically, the aberrant activity in the left middle temporal gyrus could potentially function as a marker to assess the progression of depression and evaluate the efficacy of treatments for aMDD. The left cingulate gyrus, left rolandic operculum, and certain neural circuits exhibit abnormal neural activity in females with aMDD, which may serve as a neurobiological marker. This finding also provides an imaging foundation for gender-specific treatment approaches and prognosis evaluation of aMDD in future studies. Overall, our study provides robust evidence-based medical insights into the specific neural mechanisms underlying aMDD, and supports the development of personalised treatment strategies.

sec.title: Limitation
sec.p: This study focused solely on fMRI data derived from whole-brain analyses, which necessitated the exclusion of literature that relied on ROI analysis.

The aim of this study was to identify neurobiological markers associated with anxiety-type depression. However, the analysis of neuroimaging data, which was limited to fMRI, may introduce potential biases. Future studies should consider integrating both structural and functional magnetic resonance imaging data to achieve a more comprehensive analysis.

Due to the inclusion of studies exclusively involving adult participants and the absence of detailed reports on patient education in some of the results, it was not feasible to conduct subgroup analyses based on age or educational attainment.

sec.title: Disclosure statement
sec.p: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).

ref.mixed-citation: Agosti V, Stewart JW. 2001. Atypical and non-atypical subtypes of depression: comparison of social functioning, symptoms, course of illness, co-morbidity and demographic features. J Affect Disord. 65(1):75–79. 10.1016/s0165-0327(00)00251-211426513

Albajes-Eizagirre A et al. 2019. Meta-analysis of voxel-based neuroimaging studies using seed-based d mapping with permutation of subject images (SDM-PSI). J Vis Exp. 153:e59841. 10.3791/5984131840658

Altinay M, Karne H, Beall E, Anand A. 2016. Quetiapine extended release open-label treatment associated changes in amygdala activation and connectivity in anxious depression: an fMRI study. J Clin Psychopharmacol. 36(6):562–571. 10.1097/jcp.000000000000060027768670
